nodes.name: Su Shi
nodes.description: Su Shi was a Chinese poet, writer, calligrapher, painter, gastronome, and politician of the Northern Song dynasty; one of the most celebrated literary figures in Chinese history, also known as Dongpo Jushi.

nodes.name: Su Xun
nodes.description: Father of Su Shi; prominent Northern Song literary figure and educator.

nodes.name: Su Zhe
nodes.description: Su Zhe was the younger brother of Su Shi and a Northern Song scholar-official, writer, and statesman; he was part of the renowned 'Three Sus'—a literary trio comprising their father Su Xun and the two brothers—and authored Su Shi's epitaph.

nodes.name: Cheng Shi
nodes.description: Mother of Su Shi; known for her moral instruction and use of the 'cutting-the-shuttle' parable to instill diligence.

nodes.name: Wang Fu
nodes.description: Wife of Su Shi; died young in 1065; subject of his famous elegiac poem 'Jiang Cheng Zi'.

nodes.name: Chao Yun
nodes.description: Chao Yun was Su Shi's concubine and lifelong companion who accompanied him through exile and hardship; she shared his deep Buddhist interests and served as his spiritual companion.

nodes.name: Wang Anshi
nodes.description: Northern Song statesman, reformer, and chancellor; architect of the New Policies (Xin Fa) that Su Shi opposed.

nodes.name: Ouyang Xiu
nodes.description: Northern Song scholar, historian, poet, literary master, and literary critic; served as chief examiner in the 1057 imperial examination where Su Shi excelled, and acted as Su Shi's mentor and early patron.

nodes.name: Li Ding
nodes.description: Northern Song official and poet; one of the prosecutors in the Crow Terrace Poetry Case who accused Su Shi of slander.

nodes.name: Shu Dan
nodes.description: Northern Song official and poet; co-prosecutor in the Crow Terrace Poetry Case.

nodes.name: Wang Shen
nodes.description: Northern Song painter, calligrapher, and prince; close friend and patron of Su Shi; aided in his rescue during the Crow Terrace Poetry Case.

nodes.name: Fo Yin
nodes.description: Buddhist monk and close friend of Su Shi; engaged in philosophical dialogues with him, notably the 'Eight Winds' anecdote.

nodes.name: Fan Pang
nodes.description: Historical Chinese official and moral exemplar from the Eastern Han dynasty; cited by young Su Shi as a role model for integrity and filial piety.

nodes.name: Meizhou Meishan
nodes.description: Ancient Chinese city; modern-day Meishan City, Sichuan Province; birthplace of Su Shi.

nodes.name: Bianjing
nodes.description: Capital city of the Northern Song dynasty; present-day Kaifeng, Henan Province; site of imperial examinations and central government service.

nodes.name: Hangzhou
nodes.description: City in Zhejiang Province; where Su Shi served twice as prefect and constructed the Su Causeway on West Lake.

nodes.name: Mizhou
nodes.description: City in Shandong Province; where Su Shi served as prefect and composed 'Jiang Cheng Zi · Mi Zhou Chu Lie'.

nodes.name: Xuzhou
nodes.description: City in Jiangsu Province; where Su Shi served as prefect and successfully led flood control efforts after the Yellow River breach.

nodes.name: Huangzhou
nodes.description: City and ancient administrative region in present-day Hubei Province; site of Su Shi’s first major exile (1080–1084), where he established his 'Dongpo' farm and composed seminal literary works including 'Nian Nu Jiao · Chibi Huai Gu' (also known as 'Red Cliff Rhapsody').

nodes.name: Huizhou
nodes.description: City and prefecture in southern Guangdong Province; Su Shi’s second place of exile (1094–1097), where he wrote 'Ri Dan Li Zhi San Bai Ke'; famed for its lychees and cultural isolation.

nodes.name: Dan Prefecture (Danzhou)
nodes.description: Region in present-day Hainan Province—specifically a prefecture on Hainan Island—serving as Su Shi's final and most remote exile from 1097 to 1100; regarded as the most severe banishment destination in Song-era China, where he nonetheless taught and promoted Confucian education.

nodes.name: Changzhou
nodes.description: City in Jiangsu Province; Su Shi's final residence and place of death in 1101 during his return journey after receiving imperial amnesty.

nodes.name: West Lake (Xi Hu)
nodes.description: Famous lake in Hangzhou; central to Su Shi’s public works (Su Causeway) and poetic inspiration.

nodes.name: Crow Terrace (Yu Tai)
nodes.description: Historic prison in Bianjing where Su Shi was detained during the Crow Terrace Poetry Case.

nodes.name: Su Causeway (Su Ti)
nodes.description: A famous embankment built by Su Shi in Hangzhou across West Lake during his governance (1089–1091) to control flooding and improve water transport; now one of the 'Ten Scenes of West Lake'.

nodes.name: Dongpo (Eastern Slope)
nodes.description: A hillside in Huangzhou where Su Shi farmed and adopted the sobriquet 'Dongpo Jushi'.

nodes.name: Anle Fang
nodes.description: Government-run medical institution founded by Su Shi in Hangzhou; considered China's earliest state-sponsored hospital.

nodes.name: Three Sus
nodes.description: Literary and political collective comprising Su Xun, Su Shi, and Su Zhe; all included among the 'Eight Great Prose Masters of the Tang and Song'.

nodes.name: Eight Great Prose Masters of the Tang and Song
nodes.description: Elite group of eight prose writers from the Tang and Song dynasties, recognized for their mastery of classical prose; includes all three Sus.

nodes.name: New Policies (Xin Fa)
nodes.description: Major political reform movement initiated under Emperor Shenzong of Song, led by Wang Anshi; opposed by Su Shi.

nodes.name: Crow Terrace Poetry Case (Wu Tai Shi An)
nodes.description: Infamous political persecution in 1079 targeting Su Shi over alleged seditious poetry; resulted in his imprisonment and demotion to Huangzhou.

nodes.name: 1057 Imperial Examination
nodes.description: Imperial civil service examination held in 1057 CE, notable for Su Shi's outstanding performance and Ouyang Xiu's recognition.

nodes.name: Nian Nu Jiao · Chibi Huai Gu (Remembrance of a Fair Lady · Reminiscence of Red Cliff)
nodes.description: A celebrated literary work by Su Shi composed in Huangzhou; widely regarded as the greatest ci poem in Chinese history.

nodes.name: Qian Chi Bi Fu (First Ode on the Red Cliff)
nodes.description: A renowned rhapsody (fu) written by Su Shi during his Huangzhou exile; explores themes of impermanence and transcendence through a moonlit boat trip on the Yangtze.

nodes.name: Ding Feng Bo (Calming the Storm)
nodes.description: A famous ci poem expressing resilience and equanimity amid adversity; composed in Huangzhou.

nodes.name: Jiang Cheng Zi (Tune: Riverside Town) — 'Shi Nian Sheng Si Liang Mang Mang'
nodes.description: A poignant elegy written by Su Shi in Mizhou ten years after Wang Fu's death; considered the greatest elegiac poem in Chinese literature.

nodes.name: Yin Hu Shang Chu Qing Hou Yu (Drinking on West Lake: First Sunny, Then Rainy)
nodes.description: A celebrated poem composed in Hangzhou praising West Lake's beauty; contains the famous simile comparing the lake to Xi Shi.

nodes.name: Jiang Cheng Zi · Mi Zhou Chu Lie (Riverside Town · Hunting in Mizhou)
nodes.description: A bold and energetic ci poem composed in Mizhou; exemplifies Su Shi's heroic and unrestrained lyrical style.

nodes.name: Huang Tingjian
nodes.description: Northern Song dynasty poet and calligrapher; Su Shi's contemporary and poetic counterpart ('Su-Huang').

nodes.name: Xin Qiji
nodes.description: Southern Song dynasty poet; later master of the bold and unconstrained ci style, paired with Su Shi as 'Su-Xin'.

nodes.name: Meishan
nodes.description: Historic city in Sichuan Province; birthplace and ancestral home of Su Shi and Su Zhe.

nodes.name: Wutai Poetry Case
nodes.description: Literary and political incident in 1079 that led to Su Shi's arrest and near-execution over alleged seditious poetry; precipitated his exile to Huangzhou.

nodes.name: Rhapsody on the Red Cliff (Former)
nodes.description: Major literary work composed by Su Shi in Huangzhou (c. 1082); a rhapsody reflecting on history, impermanence, and transcendence.

nodes.name: Huangzhou Cold Food Observance Poem Scroll
nodes.description: Iconic calligraphic masterpiece written by Su Shi in Huangzhou (1082); renowned as the 'Third Best Running Script Scroll' in Chinese art history.

nodes.name: Dongpo Pork
nodes.description: Famous dish traditionally attributed to Su Shi's culinary innovation during his Huangzhou exile.

edges.description: The 'Three Sus' refers to the literary and familial grouping of Su Shi, his father Su Xun, and his brother Su Zhe — jointly celebrated as foundational figures in classical Chinese prose and core members of the Eight Great Prose Masters.

The 'Eight Great Prose Masters of the Tang and Song' is a canonical literary grouping that includes Su Shi, Su Xun, Su Zhe, Ouyang Xiu, Wang Anshi, and others — representing a shared cultural-historical category of elite prose writers whose works defined classical standards.
keywords: Eight Great Prose Masters of the Tang and Song

edges.description: The 1057 Imperial Examination was a pivotal joint event where Su Shi and Su Zhe both passed the highest-level civil service exam under chief examiner Ouyang Xiu — marking their formal entry into officialdom and launching their literary-political careers together.

The Crow Terrace Poetry Case (1079) was a political prosecution targeting Su Shi’s poetry; key accusers Li Ding and Shu Dan collaborated in collecting, interpreting, and presenting verses as evidence of treasonous satire against the New Policies — making it a coordinated judicial-political action involving multiple actors.
keywords: Crow Terrace Poetry Case (Wu Tai Shi An)

edges.description: During the Yellow River flood crisis in Xuzhou (1077), Su Shi led emergency flood control efforts with local officials and laborers; though unnamed individuals are implied, the text explicitly frames this as a collective civic action — and 'Xuzhou' functions as a locative participant anchoring the event's spatial and administrative context.

Su Shi’s poetic dialogue with Fo Yin during the 'Eight Winds' anecdote reflects a sustained philosophical exchange rooted in Chan Buddhist practice — where mutual challenge, humor, and spiritual insight bind them as co-participants in a thematic hyperedge of Zen teaching and self-cultivation.

In Hangzhou (1089), Su Shi executed the West Lake dredging and dike-construction project — a large-scale public works initiative involving coordinated labor, engineering, and ecological management; 'West Lake (Xi Hu)' and 'Su Causeway (Su Ti)' are inseparable co-constitutive participants in this socio-ecological intervention.

The founding of Anle Fang in Hangzhou represents Su Shi’s institutionalization of public healthcare — a collaborative civic act linking governance, medicine, and social welfare; 'Anle Fang' and 'Hangzhou' are co-located, co-functional participants in this pioneering public health initiative.

Su Shi’s exile to Huangzhou (1080–1084) involved material hardship, intellectual flourishing, and identity transformation — crystallized in the naming of 'Dongpo (Eastern Slope)' after the land he cultivated; 'Huangzhou', 'Dongpo', and 'Su Shi' form an inseparable triadic relationship of place, self-naming, and existential redefinition.

participants: Jiang Cheng Zi (Tune: Riverside Town) — 'Shi Nian Sheng Si Liang Mang Mang'
edges.description: Su Shi’s lifelong devotion to his wife Wang Fu — memorialized in the elegiac poem 'Jiang Cheng Zi — Shi Nian Sheng Si Liang Mang Mang' — establishes a profound biographical and literary bond; 'Wang Fu' and 'Jiang Cheng Zi (Tune: Riverside Town) — 'Shi Nian Sheng Si Liang Mang Mang'' are co-constituted through grief, memory, and poetic form.
keywords: Jiang Cheng Zi (Tune: Riverside Town) — 'Shi Nian Sheng Si Liang Mang Mang'

edges.description: Chao Yun’s unwavering companionship across Su Shi’s exiles — from Huangzhou to Huizhou to Danzhou — constitutes a relational anchor of loyalty and emotional resilience; she followed him despite family opposition and served as his Buddhist intellectual companion, making them co-participants in a narrative of steadfast human connection and shared spiritual endurance.

participants: Nian Nu Jiao · Chibi Huai Gu (Remembrance of a Fair Lady · Reminiscence of Red Cliff)
Qian Chi Bi Fu (First Ode on the Red Cliff)
edges.description: The 'Nian Nu Jiao · Chibi Huai Gu' and 'Qian Chi Bi Fu' are two masterworks composed in close succession during Su Shi’s Huangzhou exile — thematically unified by Red Cliff imagery, historical reflection, and metaphysical inquiry; they jointly define his mature literary voice and constitute a paired creative output.
keywords: Nian Nu Jiao · Chibi Huai Gu (Remembrance of a Fair Lady · Reminiscence of Red Cliff)
Qian Chi Bi Fu (First Ode on the Red Cliff)

participants: Yin Hu Shang Chu Qing Hou Yu (Drinking on West Lake: First Sunny, Then Rainy)
edges.description: Su Shi’s poetic response to weather and landscape at West Lake — 'Yin Hu Shang Chu Qing Hou Yu' — embodies a synesthetic, philosophical engagement with place; 'West Lake (Xi Hu)' is not merely setting but an active aesthetic and conceptual partner in the poem’s meaning-making.
keywords: Yin Hu Shang Chu Qing Hou Yu (Drinking on West Lake: First Sunny, Then Rainy)

participants: Jiang Cheng Zi · Mi Zhou Chu Lie (Riverside Town · Hunting in Mizhou)
edges.description: The 'Jiang Cheng Zi · Mi Zhou Chu Lie' was composed during Su Shi’s tenure as Mizhou prefect — reflecting his leadership, martial ethos, and literary innovation in that locale; 'Mizhou' and the poem are co-indexed as site and artifact of civic-poetic synthesis.
keywords: Jiang Cheng Zi · Mi Zhou Chu Lie (Riverside Town · Hunting in Mizhou)

edges.description: Su Shi’s final journey north after imperial amnesty culminated in his death in Changzhou in 1101, marking the culmination of his life journey, final illness, and posthumous veneration; the city thus became a terminal node of biography, mourning, and legacy — binding 'Changzhou' and 'Su Shi' in an irreversible, historically attested endpoint relationship.

Su Shi and Su Zhe shared an exceptionally close fraternal bond, widely regarded as one of the most moving brotherly relationships in Chinese literary history; Su Zhe buried Su Shi and composed his epitaph after his death.
Su Shi and Ouyang Xiu were linked by mentorship and literary recognition: Ouyang Xiu famously praised Su Shi’s examination essays in 1057, launching his national reputation.
Su Shi and Huang Tingjian formed the core of the 'Su-Huang' poetic school; they are jointly celebrated as the two greatest poets of the Northern Song, representing the pinnacle of Song poetry.

Su Shi and Xin Qiji are canonically paired as the foundational figures of the bold and unconstrained (hàofàng) ci tradition; though from different generations, their stylistic legacy is inseparably conjoined as 'Su-Xin'.

participants: Huangzhou Cold Food Observance Poem Scroll
edges.description: Su Shi’s exile to Huangzhou (1080–1084) was a defining period marked by profound literary output—including the 'Rhapsody on the Red Cliff (Former)' and the 'Huangzhou Cold Food Observance Poem Scroll'—and personal resilience.
keywords: Huangzhou Cold Food Observance Poem Scroll

edges.description: Su Shi’s administrative service in Hangzhou included designing and overseeing construction of the Su Causeway (Su Ti), a landmark civil engineering and cultural project that transformed West Lake.

Su Shi’s 1094 exile to Huizhou inspired iconic verses ('I’ll eat three hundred lychees a day, and gladly be a southerner forever') and reflected his adaptive, joyful spirit amid political persecution.

Su Shi’s final exile to Dan Prefecture (Danzhou) in 1097—then the remotest frontier of Song China—became a symbol of his moral fortitude and cultural mission, as he taught locals and preserved Confucian-Buddhist learning.

The Wutai Poetry Case (1079) was a pivotal political prosecution targeting Su Shi’s poetry as seditious, resulting in his imprisonment and subsequent demotion to Huangzhou—the catalyst for his transformative 'eastern slope' phase.

Su Shi’s culinary innovation 'Dongpo Pork' emerged from his Huangzhou exile and endures as a cultural artifact embodying his ingenuity, warmth, and integration of daily life with artistic sensibility.
